---
title: "Redface Topminnow (Fundulus rubrifrons)"
slug: "fundulus-rubrifrons"
group: "Actinopterygii"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475900792/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Redface Topminnow"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Animal"
tax_class: "Actinopterygii"
tax_order: "Cyprinodontiformes"
tax_family: "Fundulidae"
categories: ["Animal", "Actinopterygii", "Cyprinodontiformes", "Fundulidae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475900792/medium.jpeg" alt="Redface Topminnow" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/coreytcallaghan">
coreytcallaghan</a> (CC BY-NC 4.0) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264990526">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Actinopterygii
-   **Order:** Cyprinodontiformes
-   **Family:** Fundulidae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=101070)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   **IUCN:** Least Concern
-   **Rarity note:** Common in freshwater systems

## Natural history notes

A small freshwater fish characterized by its slender body and the reddish coloration on the face and fins of breeding males. In Florida, it is found in clear, slow-moving waters such as springs, streams, and vegetated margins of rivers. It feeds on small invertebrates and is typically active near the water’s surface, especially in warm conditions.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/101070-Fundulus-rubrifrons)
-   [Local profile](https://www.floridamuseum.ufl.edu/discover-fish/florida-fishes-gallery/flagfish/)
